title: Lowest Common Ancestor of a BST
problem_statement: Find the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two given nodes in a binary search tree.

examples.explanation: 6 is the lowest node that has both 2 and 8 as descendants.

constraints: All node values are unique and p, q exist in the tree.
intuition: Use BST ordering to move left or right until the current node is between p and q or equals one of them.

approaches.name: Iterative BST traversal
approaches.description: Move down the tree using values: go left if both targets are smaller, right if both are larger, otherwise current node is LCA.
approaches.why_not: A generic binary tree LCA solution uses extra recursion and does not exploit BST properties.
interview_commentary: Use BST ordering to decide whether to go left or right.
The split point where p and q diverge is the LCA.
If one target equals the current node, it is the LCA.

approaches.name: Recursive BST traversal
approaches.description: Use recursion to descend into the appropriate subtree until the split point is reached.
approaches.why_not: Iterative traversal avoids recursion overhead and uses constant additional space.
interview_commentary: Recursively follow the BST path until the split point is found.
The first node whose children diverge contains the LCA.
BST properties let you avoid exploring both subtrees.

interview_qa.a: They let you decide which subtree contains both nodes instead of exploring both.
The first node where p and q diverge into different subtrees or one matches the current node.

interview_qa.q: Why can current root be the LCA?
interview_qa.a: Because one node is on one side and the other is on the other side, so current root is the lowest common ancestor.

interview_qa.q: What if p or q equals root?
